risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Students were then randomized to experimental or control groups, using the random blocks method21 stratifying on gender (male/female) and self-reported attention deficit hyperactivity disorder status (yes, no), to either (a) immediate instruction in the TM program or (b) wait list/delayed start condition." Comment: This describes a random block method with stratification, which indicates a proper random allocation sequence was used. The methods section provides sufficient detail to judge this as truly random allocation.

Quote: "The treatment group allocations were concealed by the study statistician, with individual treatment group assignments revealed to the project manager only when study participants completed baseline testing." Comment: This explicitly states a concealment process that prevented the recruiter/project manager from knowing assignments before baseline testing was completed, which is sufficient for low risk of allocation concealment bias.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "This was a single-blind RCT comparing stress reduction with the TM program to wait-list controls" and "The treatment group allocations were concealed ... All assessments were administered by research staff who were masked to treatment condition. Thus, this was a single-blind design." Comment: Participants received an obvious behavioral intervention (TM instruction and ongoing practice) versus wait-list control, so participants and intervention personnel were not blinded; because outcomes included self-reported psychological distress and coping, knowledge of allocation could plausibly influence them. Masking applied only to assessors, not to participants/personnel delivering the intervention.

Quote: "All assessments were administered by research staff who were masked to treatment condition. Thus, this was a single-blind design." Comment: Outcome assessment was performed by masked research staff (independent assessor/research staff), so knowledge of allocation was prevented. The main outcomes included automated blood pressure measurement and self-report psychological scales; because the assessors were blinded and the objective BP measurement was automated, there is no indication that outcome measurement could be influenced by knowledge of allocation.

Quote: "Of the 298 participants ... 207 students who completed both BP baseline and post-testing" and "A limitation is that about 30% of the pretest sample was unavailable for post-testing." Comment: Total attrition was 91/298 (30.5%) at the relevant post-test time point, which crosses the strict >=30% threshold. Although the paper states that completers did not differ from non-completers at baseline, Cochrane guidance here treats this level of missing outcome data as high risk unless a fully convincing missing-data analysis is presented; the paper reports only complete-case/ANCOVA analyses and no multiple imputation or robust sensitivity analysis.
